---
title: "Trig and Complex Numbers"
description: "How radian language, sine and cosine, and basic complex-number geometry repair the most common pre-theory gaps in oscillation and rotation language."
page-type: concept
status: published
update-status: current
level: foundation
topic: algebra-repair
prerequisites:
  - /topics/foundation/algebra-repair/concepts/functions-and-graph-reading.qmd
  - /topics/foundation/algebra-repair/concepts/exponents-logarithms-and-growth.qmd
learning-goals:
  - "Interpret sine and cosine through the unit circle."
  - "Use radians as the natural angle scale in later math."
  - "Read complex numbers in both algebraic and geometric form."
  - "Recognize rotation and oscillation as the shared language behind trigonometry and complex numbers."
applications:
  - calculus
  - signal-processing
  - dynamical-systems
keywords:
  - trigonometry
  - radians
  - sine
  - cosine
  - complex numbers
maintainer: mtuann
last-reviewed: 2026-04-25
source-check-date: 2026-04-25
review-cadence: annual
date-modified: last-modified
draft: false
related-pages:
  - /topics/foundation/algebra-repair/index.qmd
  - /topics/foundation/single-variable-calculus/index.qmd
  - /topics/advanced/odes-and-dynamical-systems/index.qmd
  - /topics/advanced/signal-processing-and-estimation/index.qmd
stable-sources:
  - "OpenStax"
  - "Khan Academy"
  - "Paul's Online Math Notes"
current-sources:
  - "OpenStax College Algebra 2e"
  - "OpenStax Precalculus 2e"
  - "Khan Academy Trigonometry"
  - "Khan Academy Precalculus"
  - "Paul's Notes Trig Functions"
  - "Paul's Notes Complex Numbers"
---

## Role

This page is the fourth step of the algebra-repair module.

It repairs another common gap that later math quietly assumes is already stable:

- radians as the default angle language
- sine and cosine as coordinates, not just triangle mnemonics
- complex numbers as points in the plane, not symbolic exceptions
- rotation and oscillation as shared geometric structure

## First-Pass Promise

Read this page after [Exponents, Logarithms, and Growth](exponents-logarithms-and-growth.qmd).

If you stop here, you should still understand:

- why radians are the natural unit for angle
- how sine and cosine come from the unit circle
- how complex numbers extend the real line into a plane
- why multiplication by special complex numbers acts like rotation and scaling
- why this page helps later calculus, signals, and dynamical systems

## Why It Matters

Trigonometry and complex numbers look like separate topics at first, but later math keeps using them together.

They appear in:

- periodic motion
- waves and signals
- rotations in the plane
- oscillatory solutions of differential equations
- Fourier and spectral language

When this layer is weak, later formulas involving `sin`, `cos`, `e^{i\theta}`, or oscillation often feel much more mysterious than they need to.

## Prerequisite Recall

- [Functions and Graph Reading](functions-and-graph-reading.qmd) already treated `sin` and `cos` as functions, not only buttons on a calculator
- [Exponents, Logarithms, and Growth](exponents-logarithms-and-growth.qmd) already built the habit of reading functions structurally
- coordinates in the plane give geometric meaning to algebraic objects

## Intuition

The unit circle is the cleanest first-pass picture for trigonometry.

If an angle $\theta$ lands on a point of the unit circle, then that point has coordinates

$$
(\cos \theta, \sin \theta).
$$

So cosine and sine are not arbitrary formulas. They are coordinates of rotation.

Complex numbers then continue the same story.

The number

$$
a+bi
$$

can be read as a point in the plane:

$$
(a,b).
$$

That means complex arithmetic is not just symbolic play. It carries geometry:

- addition shifts points
- magnitude measures distance from the origin
- special multiplications encode rotation and scaling

## Formal Core

::: {#def-radian}
## Definition: Radian

An angle of one radian is the angle that cuts off an arc of length `1` on the unit circle.

Radians are the natural angle unit because they tie angle directly to arc length and make later calculus formulas clean.
:::

::: {#def-unit-circle}
## Definition: Unit-Circle Trigonometry

On the unit circle, the point at angle $\theta$ has coordinates

$$
(\cos \theta, \sin \theta).
$$

This is the main first-pass meaning of cosine and sine.
:::

::: {#def-complex-number}
## Definition: Complex Number

A complex number has the form

$$
z = a + bi,
$$

where $a$ and $b$ are real and

$$
i^2 = -1.
$$

Its real part is $a$ and its imaginary part is $b$.
:::

::: {#def-modulus}
## Definition: Modulus

The modulus of

$$
z = a+bi
$$

is

$$
|z| = \sqrt{a^2+b^2}.
$$

Geometrically, this is the distance from the origin to the point $(a,b)$.
:::

::: {#thm-unit-circle-identity}
## Theorem Idea: Unit-Circle Identity

Because points on the unit circle have distance `1` from the origin,

$$
\cos^2 \theta + \sin^2 \theta = 1.
$$

This identity is geometric before it is algebraic.
:::

::: {#thm-complex-geometry}
## Theorem Idea: Complex Numbers Carry Geometry

When a complex number is viewed as a point in the plane:

- addition acts like vector addition
- modulus gives distance from the origin
- multiplying by a complex number of modulus `1` corresponds to rotation

This is the bridge from algebraic symbols into oscillation and rotation language.
:::

## Worked Example

Let

$$
z = 1+i.
$$

### Step 1: Read it geometrically

The point corresponding to $z$ is

$$
(1,1).
$$

### Step 2: Compute the modulus

$$
|z| = \sqrt{1^2+1^2} = \sqrt{2}.
$$

So the point lies a distance $\sqrt{2}$ from the origin.

### Step 3: Connect to angle language

The point $(1,1)$ lies on the line $y=x$, so its direction from the origin is an angle of

$$
\frac{\pi}{4}
$$

or `45` degrees.

### Step 4: Normalize to the unit circle

If we divide by its modulus, we get

$$
\frac{1+i}{\sqrt{2}}.
$$

This point lies on the unit circle and has coordinates

$$
\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right).
$$

So

$$
\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}.
$$

This example matters because it shows the same object being read three ways:

- algebraically as `1+i`
- geometrically as `(1,1)`
- trigonometrically as a direction with angle $\pi/4$

## Computation Lens

For first-pass trig and complex work, the main habits are:

1. prefer radians when the goal is later math, not only geometry homework
2. read `sin` and `cos` from the unit-circle coordinate picture
3. translate complex numbers into points in the plane immediately
4. compute modulus before trying to interpret scale
5. move back and forth between algebraic and geometric views instead of choosing only one

This page is mainly about making later formulas feel interpretable rather than magical.

## Application Lens

This page feeds directly into:

- [Single-Variable Calculus](../../single-variable-calculus/index.qmd) through trig derivatives and periodic behavior
- [ODEs and Dynamical Systems](../../../advanced/odes-and-dynamical-systems/index.qmd) through oscillation and modes
- [Signal Processing and Estimation](../../../advanced/signal-processing-and-estimation/index.qmd) through waves, spectra, and sinusoidal language

It is also one of the clearest places where geometry and algebra stop being separate subjects.

## Stop Here For First Pass

If you can now explain:

- why radians are the natural angle unit
- how `\sin \theta` and `\cos \theta` come from the unit circle
- how to read `a+bi` as a point
- what modulus measures
- why trig and complex numbers share a common rotation story

then this page has done its job.

## Go Deeper

The next page in this module is:

- [Symbol Manipulation Lab](symbol-manipulation-lab.qmd)

The strongest adjacent bridges are:

- [Exponents, Logarithms, and Growth](exponents-logarithms-and-growth.qmd)
- [Single-Variable Calculus](../../single-variable-calculus/index.qmd)
- [Signal Processing and Estimation](../../../advanced/signal-processing-and-estimation/index.qmd)

## Optional Deeper Reading After First Pass

- [OpenStax Precalculus 2e](https://openstax.org/details/books/precalculus-2e) - open official bridge text for trigonometric and complex-number language. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Khan Academy Trigonometry](https://www.khanacademy.org/math/trigonometry) - official practice-oriented reinforcement for unit-circle and radian fluency. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Paul's Notes Complex Numbers](https://tutorial.math.lamar.edu/Classes/Alg/ComplexNumbers.aspx) - stable worked-example page for the algebraic and geometric basics of complex numbers. Checked `2026-04-25`.

## Sources and Further Reading

- [OpenStax Precalculus 2e](https://openstax.org/details/books/precalculus-2e) - `First pass` - open official reference for trigonometric functions, radians, and complex-number basics. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [OpenStax College Algebra 2e](https://openstax.org/details/books/college-algebra-2e) - `Second pass` - open official algebra bridge for symbolic fluency around these topics. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Khan Academy Trigonometry](https://www.khanacademy.org/math/trigonometry) - `First pass` - official skill-based reinforcement for unit-circle intuition and trig functions. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Khan Academy Precalculus](https://www.khanacademy.org/math/precalculus) - `Second pass` - broader official practice hub for trigonometric and complex-number fluency. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Paul's Notes Trig Functions](https://tutorial.math.lamar.edu/Classes/Alg/TrigFcns.aspx) - `First pass` - stable worked-example page for trig-function interpretation. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Paul's Notes Complex Numbers](https://tutorial.math.lamar.edu/Classes/Alg/ComplexNumbers.aspx) - `First pass` - stable worked-example page for algebraic and geometric complex-number basics. Checked `2026-04-25`.

Sources checked online on `2026-04-25`:

- OpenStax Precalculus 2e
- OpenStax College Algebra 2e
- Khan Academy Trigonometry
- Khan Academy Precalculus
- Paul's Notes Trig Functions
- Paul's Notes Complex Numbers
